data.title: September Readers ID'd 17 Ideal 'Safer' Dividends In 39 Dogs
data.description:
Reader Selections

Since May, 2017, any dividend-paying stock mentioned in a message, e-mail or comment to the author has been fair game for a reader favorite listing in this series of articles. Thus, It is possible that only rogues and discontinued, or dreadful, doubtful, dividend issues may appear.

Most valuable for the writer, however, are reader comments that catch errors in calculations or changes in direction, and figures to communicate. Examples include the reader who missed my "safer" dividend follow-up articles because they contain dividend payout ratios.

However the main subject of these articles are your stock ticker comments whether they be about favorites or rogues. Be sure to mention a ticker when you comment.

The “safer” lists are now available on the regularly published side of Seeking Alpha but that has added to confusion, since my IDEAL stocks come in two flavors: (1) not so safe and (2) safe, which is preferable since the dividends are paid from cash flow, not borrowed money.

In June a reader asked if Qualified dividend stocks could be flagged. YCharts is taking that request up with their product development team. No word has come from YCharts, yet.

My ten-year collection of Dogs of The Week portfolios are reserved for members of my Seeking Alpha Dividend Dogcatcher investing group subscribers.

Still, every month readers have grumbled that they can’t find my ten Dog of the week portfolios in my Dividend Dogcatcher service on the SA Marketplace (now called Investing Groups) site.

Summary and reference guides to 10 portfolios:

This year SA has listed all my summaries of portfolio postings in my Dogcatcher Investing Group, by date:

In Published site:

August 22, 2015 for I

October 13, 2016 for II

September 12, 2017 for III

September 13, 2018 for IV (Ivy)

September 8, 2019 for V (Volio)

September 12, 2020 for VI (Vista)

October 10, 2021 for VII (Viital)

October 8, 2022 for VIII (Viking)

October 7, 2023 for IX (Ignition)

October 11, 2024 for X (Attention)

In Investing Groups site:

October 6, 2025 for XI (Elephant)

Incidentally, My Ignition (DOTW IX) portfolio launched October 6, 2023, had all its selections in place by October 4, 2024 and posted its last return on October 3, 2025.

The Attention Portfolio (DOTW X) was filled October 10 2025 and will be fully funded by October 9, 2026.

The Elephant Folio (DOTW XI) initiated October 6. 2025 will be filled with 52 member stocks in October 9, 2026, and be fully funded by October 8, 2027.

Stay tuned for the first newly formatted Toy Dog Portfolio of tiny but mighty low priced dividend dogs.

Last winter a reader asked what the XN rating in the Market Cap column of my Yield and Target Price charts means. Here is my summary of those codes:

Dogcatcher Market Cap Codes

L= $10B+

M= $2B-$10B

S= $200M-$2B

XN=<$200M

Your comments count and encourage better writing by the dogcatcher

An observation from April 2023:

“Mr. Arnold, I enjoy reading your many articles and get some great ideas from them. However I find the number of charts are a bit of overkill, many duplicating the info. I'm sure you have your set format and reasons why, but I'd be happy with only two charts; your sort by target gains and your sort by div yield. It would make it a much easier read. Thank You —pmbrandt 15Apr2023.”

I’d gladly just submit those two charts and ditch all the verbiage. However, fifteen years ago when I started submitting articles, the SA editorial team said they needed text to go with the graphics and topsy was born.

Still, there are frequent complaints of too many words for little information. In May, somebody thought I was paid by the word. No such luck.

In May. 2025 a reader, named Move on, commented on my Bloomberg Focus List article, “Death by data…just get to the point already…"

My reply: @Move on Good point! Here's a hint: Forget the text. Read the charts and graphs. Those are the dots to connect.

In January 2025 I got in trouble with readers when I neglected to define an acronym used to describe the Ben Graham Value collection. Unintroduced, I dropped a GASV acronym on them which stood for “Graham All-Star Value” stocks, dogs, or pack.

No doubt, less is more! I’ll be alert for methods to make my Dividend Kings, Dow Dogs, Aristocrats, Fortune 500 Industry Leaders, MoPays, Portfolios and all my other articles, less obfuscating.

In February, 2022 one reader suggested an option strategy for monthly-paying dividend stocks:

"You should identify where options are available on the Monthly dividend stocks. What I do is find mo-pay stocks with options, I buy and write covered calls about 6 - 12 months out. I look for a scenario where I collect the dividend and get my stock bought back at a much lower price than I pay, but pocket a premium that makes up the loss. This gives me a dividend boost, since my cost is lower. It's like a guaranteed CD with little risk."

[See a summary of ten fair-priced September ReFa/Ro Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and twenty-eight “safer” choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

More than one writer has decried my favoritism for low-priced stocks. They especially dislike my "ideal" stocks whose dividend returns from a $1k investment equal or exceed share price.

A prime example is Sirius XM Holdings Inc (SIRI); the satellite radio and pandora music catalog owner, recently spun off from Liberty media (and now listed as one of the S&P500).

A reverse-split re-priced SIRI in October 2024. Recently priced at $21.32, SIRI still easily passes my test (of dividends from $1k invested exceeding share price) with a forward dividend yield of 4.94%! A little under $1k investment now buys 46.9 shares. and they'll throw the owner a return (from that $1k invested) over 2 times the share price. Siri, however, is in the doghouse this month because of negative return for the year over 50%.

Assuming all things remain equal, SIRI dividends alone will pay back their purchase price in about 20.24 years (and that assumes the satellite radio and subscription music services can survive that long)!

In December 2023, a reader suggested my share price equal or less than the income from a $1k investment was too low. I agree with him that opportunities are lost and that many strong stocks exist at higher prices. For example, even the dividend from $10k invested in NVIDIA Corp could not buy one share of that stock. Plainly nobody buys NVDA for the dividend, just growth. (On 10/3/25 $1.00 of NVDA dividend was priced at $4,525.00)

However, I was reminded of a comment in May, 2024 that some readers take “dividends from one $1K invested” to mean the annual return from just one share. To clarify: if the single share price of a stock is $20 and the annual yield is 3.00%, the dividend paid from $1.000.00 invested is $30.00 or 1.5 times the single share price.

February 17, 2025 reader wdjaxOn responded to my suggestion that besides waiting for the market prices to drop or higher dividends to be declared for stocks, that stock splits could also make dividends from $1k invested exceed the single share price of the stock.

Stay tuned. I now include stock splits in my suggestions to those who determine the price of dividends.

Here is a useful reader report from CEFreeman.com as of May, 2025:

“Interesting! Not only have I discovered I am an accidental Income Investor, but evidently I am also a Dog Catcher. If I am understanding this correctly. I don't have $1000 to invest each time, but I strive to buy enough of something so the dividends always buy 1 share or more. Usually, I try to buy 100 shares. Sure, the positions I buy are very low priced, but I must be doing something right. I am growing my income solidly, penny by penny! Dog Catching Income Investor. I like it.

The point of this ReFa/Ro article is: “Be More Dog!” Stay tuned, Toy dog stock breeds may be coming for you!

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 30.84% To 70.61% Net Gains From Top-Ten ReFa/Ro Dogs By September, 2026.

Six of ten top ReFa/Ro Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this September, 2025 yield-based forecast for ReFa/Ro dogs, as graded by Wall St. Wizard estimates, was 60% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to September, 2026 were:

MFA Financial (MFA) was projected to net $706.14, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 8 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 70% greater than the market as a whole.

Hafnia Ltd (HAFN) was projected to net $557.27, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 70% opposite the market as a whole.

Energy Transfer (ET) was projected to net $424.97, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 18 analysts, less broker fees.The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 22% under the market as a whole.

Interpublic Group (IPG) was projected to net $416.65, based on annual dividends, the median of target price estimates from 18 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 24% under the market as a whole.

Gladstone Commercial Corp (GOOD) was projected to net $401.26 based on the median of target price estimates from 5 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 11% over the market as a whole.

LyondellBasell Industries (LYB) was projected to net $346.19, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 20 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 12% over the market as a whole.

Amcor (AMCR) was projected to net $336.91, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 12 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 27% less than the market as a whole.

Robert Half Inc (RHI) was projected to net $326.40 based on the median of target prices estimated by 11 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 3% greater than the market as a whole.

Kenvue (KVUE) was projected to net $316.93 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. A Beta number was not available for KVUE.

Dow Inc (DOW) was projected to net $308.43, based on the median of target price estimates from 20 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 12% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 41.41% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten ReFa/Ro stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 20% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs."

The September, 2025 ReFa/Ro By Total Returns

The September, 2025 ReFa/Ro By Analyst Targets

The September, 2025 ReFa/Ro By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top September ReFa/Ro Stocks By Yield Ranged 7.24% To 15.26% Per YCharts

Top ten ReFa/Ro Dogs occupied seven of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Two financial representatives placed first, and third: MFA Financial (MFA) [1], and Chimera Investment (CIM) [3].

One materials member took second, LyondellBasell [2]. Then two real estate members placed fourth, and sixth, Gladstone Commercial [4], and Modiv Industrial (MDV) [6].

Then two energy members placed fifth and seventh, Hafnia [5] and Energy Transfer [7].

There followed two consumer staples representatives in the eighth and ninth slots, Flowers Foods (FLO) [8], and Conagra Brands (CAG) [9].

Finally the industrials representative grabbed tenth, Robert Half [10], to complete the September top ten ReFa/Ro Dog team by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The top pie chart above shows the relative strengths of the top ten September ReFa/Ro dogs by dividend and price as of market close 10/14/2025.

For September all of the top-ten ReFa/Ro dogs claimed ideal status because their share prices are lower than the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in them.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share prices. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by all ten.

39 ReFa/Ro Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 10/14/25

The lower chart reveals 29 more ReFa/Ro Dividend dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock. Two the thirty-nine August ReFa/Ro selections paid no dividends.

The least expensive dog of the September ReFa/Ro was MFA Financial at $6.41 per dividend dollar and the most expensive was Apple Inc (AAPL) at $238.27.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Eleven of Thirty-nine ReFa/Ro Showed Negative Free Cash Flow Margins or paid No Dividends

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends.

The nine cash poor are: MFA Financial, LyondellBasell Industries, United Parcel (UPS), Chimera Investment, Enterprise Products (EPD), Dow, Amcor, Hormel (HRL), Coca-Cola (KO), and NextEra (NEE). Those nine were followed by two non-dividend payers, Berkshire Hathaway, and Capital Southwest.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, executive over-reach, tariffs, uncertainty, wars, confusion, and intimidation, too.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 23.87% To 56.01% Top Ten ReFa/Ro Upsides To September 2026; (32). One -2.45% Downsider Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast An 18.13% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 September ReFa/Ro Dogs As of 10/14/25 Data

Ten-top ReFa/Ro dogs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten ReFa/Ro dogs selected 10/14/25 by yield, represented seven of eleven sectors.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield ReFa/Ro Dogs (34) To Deliver 45.30% Vs. (35) 38.35% Net Gains by All Ten, Come September, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten ReFa/Ro Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 18.13% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The second lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, MFA Financial, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 71.61%.

The five lowest-priced ReFa/Ro top-yield dogs for September were: Hafnia; MFA Financial; Gladstone Commercial; Flowers; Chimera Investment, with prices ranging from $5.56 to $13.02.

Five higher-priced ReFa/Ro top-yield dogs as of September were: Modiv; Energy Transfer; Conagra; Robert Half; LyondellBasell, with prices ranging from $14.46 to $46.66.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the summer Hamptons euphoria in the markets dies, the wall street moguls get back to work, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential pronouncements gain momentum through 2025 with the exception of very few hot tech stocks, look for most of the ReFa/Ro Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or suddenly, with an assist from trade war clouds, or a pandemic of one sort or another.

Afterword

Buy Any 17 IDEAL of 28 ‘Safer' September ReFa/Ro Dogs

August, 2025, of the 39 ReFa/Ro, 28 were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields.

These seventeen ”Ideal” candidates from the “safer” group showed dividend yields from $1,000 invested exceeding their single share prices.

Contributors:

Here is the full pack of 39 September ReFa/Ro

(Listed alphabetically by ticker symbol, the pack includes the nicknames of recommending readers.)

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Re/Fa/Ro Updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" ReFa/Ro dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

data.title: Hubbell: A Sound Deal, After A Challenged Year
data.description:
About a year ago, I called shares of Hubbell (NYSE:HUBB) too electrified, despite a great positioning. These included megatrends like energy efficiency, digital technology and while the long term positioning was sound, the organic performance felt a bit soft to my taste.

With the company resorting to a >$1 billion deal for Northern Star last fall, while being valued at an earnings multiples in the high-twenties, I urged for a word of caution amidst demanding valuation and softer organic performance.

So far this year, organic growth has been disappointing (again) as expectations have come down a bit as Hubbell is announcing another interesting deal here.

An Interesting Business

Hubbell is a company which focuses on mission-critical and engineered product and solutions, often involving technology and engineering. The company was founded back in 1888 and over time has grown to generate over $5 billion in sales from utility and electrical solutions, with the utility part of the business having become the dominant segment.

These mission-critical solutions are key to customers, as reliability and safety are more determining factors than price, which means that quality is important and suppliers can fetch premium prices and thus margins. This comes as components are bought with less consideration to price, and in some cases switching costs are very high and/or alternatives are hard to find.

Product applications include a wide variety of use cases, related to energy efficiency, digital technology, urbanization, but certainly also utility applications and related to that, the world of datacenters and AI.

The combination of these megatrends proliferating themselves and inflation made that 2023 sales rose by 18% to just shy of $5 billion, with GAAP operating profits reported at 14% of sales. M&A activity and continued momentum made that 2023 sales rose another 8% to $4.37 billion, but the real achievement has been a near unprecedented 5% jump in GAAP operating margins to 19% of sales.

By the fall of last year, the company updated the full year 2025 earnings guidance to a midpoint of $16.45 per share (based on adjusted earnings) and while reported sales growth was solid, that was entirely aided by M&A efforts, with organic sales trending flattish. Trading at $430 per share, a resulting 27 times earnings multiple made me a bit cautious, in between softer organic growth (amidst a sound positioning) as well as the fact that operating margins were historically very high already.

Net debt of $1.4 billion was no issue, trending close to par with reported EBITDA at the time. My fear was that if some operating margin reversal (read contraction) might take place, is that earnings multiples would shoot up even further, all of which made the situation a bit risky, also amidst lackluster organic growth.

Stagnation

Since this time last year, shares of Hubbell have been lagging, now trading at $430 per share, thereby trading down around ten dollars from this period last year, as shares dipped alongside the market in the low $300s during the spring. Nonetheless, the slight pullback marks a serious underperformance versus the wider markets, which have seen modest gains compared to this point in time last year.

In the end, Hubbell grew 2024 sales by nearly 5% to $5.63 billion, largely driven by M&A efforts. GAAP operating margins were almost at par compared to the year before at 19%, with GAAP earnings up thirty-two cents to $14.37 per share, as adjusted earnings rose by more than a dollar to $16.57 per share.

After ending the year on a soft note, in terms of a 3% decline in organic sales, the company nonetheless issued a total and organic sales growth guidance which called for a 4-5% increase in sales in 2025. Adjusted earnings were seen moving up to a midpoint of $17.60 per share, with the adjustments largely seen in relation to amortization charges.

The company started the year on a softer note, with reported sales down 2% as organic revenue declines came in at nearly 1%, as the company maintained the guidance on all fronts. Following a 2% increase in second quarter organic sales, the midpoint of the full year adjusted earnings guidance was hiked to $17.90 per share. However, the quality of this hike is poor. After all, a change in the accounting methodology to FIFO accounting boosted earnings in the first half of the year by $0.29 per share.

Net debt was reported at $1.35 billion, mostly to fund some buybacks and with adjusted EBITDA reported $662 million in the first half of the year, that results in a leverage ratio around 1 times. With 54 million shares trading around $430 per share, the market value of the firm stands at $23.2 billion, for a $24.5 billion enterprise valuation.

Another Deal, Needed To Drive Appeal

Following lackluster performance in the first half of the year, in which I see real risks to the full year guidance, the company resorted to further M&A to provide an impetus to the business. In August, Hubbell announced the purchase of DMC Power, a provider of connector and tooling to utility substation and transmission markets.

The deal values DMC at $825 million as the company generates $130 million in revenues, on which it reports a very impressive $60 million in EBITDA. This values the business at a demanding 6.3 times sales multiple, yet this comes amidst superior margins, with the business valued at around 13.8 times EBITDA.

In comparison, the own business is valued around a high-teens EBITDA multiple, suggesting that the deal looks quite accretive, taking place at a substantial discount, while the positioning of DMC looks very strong. Pro forma net debt is seen close to $2.2 billion and with pro forma EBITDA around $1.4 billion, leverage remains very reasonable, while EBITDA accretion is seen at 4-5%.

With the deal closing on October 1st, the deal is set to be accretive to the quarterly results, as I see a credible roadmap for earnings around $18 per share. Trading at $430 per share, multiples have compressed from 27-28 to 23-24 times earnings, marking real multiple compression, yet the company continues to perform on a soft note here, with organic growth not being impressive, in fact, the contrary.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: Natural Gas Midstream Exposure With A Reasonable Entry Price
data.description:
Kinder Morgan, Inc. (NYSE:KMI) is one of the largest midstream companies in the United States, boasting an infrastructure network consisting of pipelines, storage facilities, natural gas processing plants, and hydrocarbon terminals stretching across nearly all of the continental United States:

As we can see here, the company boasts approximately 66,000 miles of natural gas pipelines, 9,500 miles of refined products pipelines, and 1,500 miles of carbon dioxide pipelines. The transportation and processing of natural gas is by far the company’s largest individual business unit, though, as it accounts for approximately 65% of the company’s projected total adjusted segment EBDA for the full-year 2025 period.

We can also see the importance of the company’s natural gas segment in its second-quarter 2025 earnings results. For that quarter, the company stated that its natural gas pipelines had a segment EBDA of $1.436 billion. This was by far the largest figure that any of the company’s business units reported:

Business Unit

Segment EBDA

Natural Gas Pipelines

$1,436

Products Pipelines

$289

Terminals

$300

CO2

$150

(All figures in millions of U.S. dollars.)

The total of all of these figures is $2.175 billion, which means that the natural gas pipeline unit accounted for 66.02% of the total. That is pretty close to the company’s expectations that this unit accounts for roughly 65% of its segment EBDA. This apparent focus on natural gas could prove to give the company an advantage over some of the other companies in the midstream sector due to the fact that natural gas has far superior prospects for forward demand growth than does crude oil, which limits the growth potential of those midstream companies that primarily own crude oil pipelines (see here).

While Kinder Morgan does own some crude oil pipelines (included in the “Products Pipelines” segment), they account for a much smaller proportion of the company’s total revenue and cash flow than they do for a company such as Plains All American Pipeline (PAA) that is almost exclusively focused on crude oil. As such, the limited growth prospects for oil are less likely to be a drag on Kinder Morgan’s growth than would be the case for some other midstream companies. In fact, the company’s already sizable presence in the natural gas midstream market could provide it with some significant forward growth opportunities, which we will discuss in this article.

Kinder Morgan – Size And Peer Comparison

Kinder Morgan is one of the more well-known names in the midstream space, which might be due to the company’s fairly rapid growth following its founding in 1997. As Kinder Morgan’s website states:

In 1997, Richard D. Kinder and William V. Morgan built an energy company by utilizing the master limited partnership financial structure as a growth vehicle, something that had never been done before.

The company that is now known as Kinder Morgan was originally founded as Kinder Morgan Partners, a master limited partnership that had an enterprise value of $350 million in 1997. The company’s enterprise value today is a much more impressive $94.50 billion, a 22.13% compound annual growth rate sustained over a period of 28 years.

Kinder Morgan is no longer a master limited partnership, having abandoned the structure in 2014 when oil prices collapsed and the unit prices of many MLPs began to falter. However, the company achieved this structural change by having KMI, the only C-corporation in the Kinder Morgan structure, purchase all of the partnership units of the various master limited partnerships. Through this action, all of the assets that were owned by the master limited partnership became part of a single publicly traded C-corporation.

Kinder Morgan’s size compares fairly favorably to other midstream master limited partnerships and corporations, but it is not the largest company in the space. We can see that in this comparison table:

Company Name

Market Capitalization

Enterprise Value

Kinder Morgan, Inc.

$60.46 billion

$94.50 billion

Energy Transfer (ET)

$57.26 billion

$133.02 billion

Enterprise Products Partners (EPD)

$65.50 billion

$98.71 billion

MPLX (MPLX)

$49.46 billion

$70.01 billion

The Williams Companies (WMB)

$76.36 billion

$106.43 billion

ONEOK (OKE)

$42.33 billion

$74.78 billion

Enbridge (ENB)

$103.00 billion

$181.02 billion

While large, we can immediately see that several of the largest midstream companies operating today are larger than Kinder Morgan either of terms of market capitalization or enterprise value. This might not be a surprise to long-time followers of the midstream industry, given that several companies shown above have been involved in the recent wave of consolidation that has swept the industry and increased the size of the surviving companies.

Of course, size is not necessarily everything, and a company does not have to be the largest in the industry to earn money and provide its investors with an attractive yield. For its part, Kinder Morgan does fairly well in the yield department, as it boasts a 4.30% yield, which is substantially higher than the 1.10% yield on the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). However, this yield is fairly low compared to what other midstream companies possess, as we can see in this chart:

Company Name

Current Yield

Kinder Morgan, Inc.

4.30%

Energy Transfer

7.91%

Enterprise Products Partners

7.21%

MPLX

7.88%

The Williams Companies

3.20%

ONEOK

6.13%

Enbridge

5.69%

We can see here that only one of the seven peer companies – The Williams Companies – has a lower current yield than Kinder Morgan. There are a few possible reasons for this, one of which is the fact that Kinder Morgan is a C-corporation, whereas Energy Transfer, Enterprise Products Partners, and MPLX are all master limited partnerships. One of the defining characteristics of MLPs is that MLPs do not pay taxes on their earnings as long as 90% of their income comes from certain sources (operating pipelines is one allowable source).

A C-corporation such as Kinder Morgan does not have this advantage, and as such, the taxes that it has to pay reduce the amount of cash that the company has available to it. Another possible reason for Kinder Morgan’s low yield relative to its peers is that the majority of its earnings come from its natural gas segment. As we have seen with The Williams Companies (which was by far the best performing of the seven companies over the past five years), the market appears to recognize that natural gas is likely to experience significant demand growth going forward and has started to price that demand growth into midstream stocks. After all, growth in natural gas consumption will require midstream companies to transport greater volumes of natural gas, and there is a direct correlation between transported resource volumes and revenues for midstream companies.

Thus, those midstream companies with a significant focus on natural gas are likely to experience strong revenue growth going forward, and the market has priced this growth into their equity prices, reducing their current yields.

Recent Performance Update

We previously discussed Kinder Morgan on August 5, 2025. The American equity market in general has been fairly strong since that time, as the S&P 500 Index has risen by 5.24% since that date:

However, oil prices (CL1:COM) have been much more subdued, which can be important given that the equity prices of midstream companies frequently exhibit a correlation to crude oil prices even though their revenues and cash flows are not affected much by crude oil price fluctuations. This is particularly true for Kinder Morgan, which only has about 5% of its cash flows exposed to commodity price movements:

As such, a rational investor would not think that crude oil prices would affect the company’s share price, but historically this has been the case. As we can see here, the spot price of West Texas Intermediate crude oil is down 11.76% since our last discussion on Kinder Morgan:

Despite Kinder Morgan having overall minimal exposure to crude oil prices, we can still expect this to apply downward pressure on Kinder Morgan’s share price over the last few weeks, especially since late September when crude oil prices began falling in earnest.

Kinder Morgan’s share price has declined since our last discussion, but it overall did hold up a lot better than the price of West Texas Intermediate crude oil:

As we can see, Kinder Morgan’s shares are down 2.96% since our last discussion, far worse than the performance of the S&P 500 Index over the same period. This seems almost certain to be rather discouraging for potential investors. After all, nobody likes to lose money. Fortunately, though, many people who have invested in Kinder Morgan have done so for the dividend, and that certainly remains intact. The long-term thesis for the natural gas unit remains intact as well, so overall, the thesis that we presented previously does remain solid. Investors should be prepared for further near-term volatility in the shares, though, as crude oil prices are likely to remain suppressed for a while, and this will apply downward pressure on the company’s shares.

About Kinder Morgan

As mentioned earlier, Kinder Morgan is one of the largest midstream companies in the United States, boasting a network of pipelines, storage facilities, natural gas processing plants, and hydrocarbon terminals that stretches across much of the United States. This gives the company a nearly unparalleled reach, as its infrastructure includes almost every major producing basin in the country.

Kinder Morgan is also one of the few pipeline operators that has gotten into the liquefied natural gas production sub-sector of the American energy industry. Kinder Morgan is one of the owners of the Elba Island LNG plant in Georgia, which produces approximately 2.5 million metric tonnes of liquefied natural gas per year and sells the product to Shell (SHEL). Kinder Morgan is also one of the partners in the Gulf LNG Liquefaction project, which has a nameplate capacity of 10.85 million tonnes of liquefied natural gas per year. The Gulf LNG Liquefaction project has not yet been completed, and last year, the partners were given until July 16, 2029, to complete it. The development of this particular project was delayed from its original expected completion in 2024, with the delay being blamed on the COVID-19 pandemic. It is, however, unclear when this liquefaction plant will actually come online as Kinder Morgan does not mention it at all in its most recent investor presentation.

As we saw earlier, Kinder Morgan derives the bulk of its earnings from its natural gas pipeline business. For 2025, the company has projected that approximately 65% of its segment EBDA would come from this business, and this is in line with the actual amount that the company reported for the second quarter of 2025. It is likely that the proportion of the company’s cash flows that comes from this business unit will increase going forward due to the fact that the majority of Kinder Morgan’s growth capital expenditures are being directed to various projects within the natural gas business. We can see that here:

As we can see, Kinder Morgan currently has a total project backlog of $9.292 billion. This is the amount that the company has budgeted for all of the growth projects that it currently has under development, regardless of the exact stage that a project is in. We also see that $7.496 billion, or 80.67%, of the current budgeted spending is devoted to various projects that are intended to support the growing national demand for natural gas. However, even this percentage understates the actual spending that the company is directing towards the natural gas space, as much of the $1.135 billion budgeted for gathering and processing is intended for the construction of natural gas gathering pipelines that will allow upstream producers to increase their natural gas production. Thus, we should probably include the gathering and processing budget in with the natural gas one, which brings us to $8.631 billion, or 92.89% of the total growth capital expenditures being directed towards natural gas projects. This seems certain to increase the proportion of Kinder Morgan’s segment EBDA that comes from natural gas going forward.

Natural Gas Fundamentals

When we look at the fundamentals for natural gas, it makes a lot of sense that Kinder Morgan would be focusing its efforts on increasing its ability to transport and store this particular compound. According to Wood Mackenzie, the demand for natural gas in the United States will increase by 22 billion cubic feet per day by the end of this decade. That is a 20% increase above the current level. Furthermore, Wood Mackenzie estimates that liquefied natural gas plants alone will require approximately 28 billion cubic feet of natural gas per day to serve as feedstock for their operations by 2030. That is a 119% increase over the 2024 consumption level of these facilities:

Kinder Morgan is positioned to be an active supplier of natural gas to the numerous liquefaction plants that are currently under construction along the Gulf Coast in Texas and Louisiana. The company currently has contracts in place with the owners of some of the liquefaction plants currently in existence to supply 8 billion cubic feet of natural gas per day to their facilities along the coast. The company also has secured contracts with companies that are still working to bring their plants to an operational state to supply them with natural gas at a future date. Based on these contracts as well as the contracts that will take effect over the next three years, we can conclude that Kinder Morgan will be supplying approximately 12 billion cubic feet of natural gas per day to the emerging liquefied natural gas export industry. That is a 50% increase in three years, and that is just one of the growth opportunities that Kinder Morgan has. The opportunities that we discussed in previous articles on this company are also still present, and this all points to Kinder Morgan having the potential to grow its natural gas business going forward.

Valuation

Analysts currently expect Kinder Morgan to report an earnings per share of $1.28 for the full-year 2025 period and $1.37 for the full-year 2026 period:

This gives the company a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 21.21 at the current stock price. This is somewhat better than the current price-to-earnings ratio for the S&P 500 Index:

However, Kinder Morgan is somewhat expensive compared to many of its peers. We can see that in this chart:

Company Name

Forward P/E Ratio

Kinder Morgan, Inc.

21.21

Energy Transfer

11.92

Enterprise Products Partners

11.27

MPLX

11.05

The Williams Companies

29.43

ONEOK

12.45

Enbridge

21.94

We can immediately see that Kinder Morgan is significantly more expensive than all three of the MLPs. This is not really surprising given that C-corporations usually have higher valuations than master limited partnerships. Kinder Morgan is also significantly more expensive than ONEOK, which has been under a lot of selling pressure recently. However, it is still cheaper than Enbridge and The Williams Companies. The Williams Companies is also very natural gas-focused, so this could mean that Kinder Morgan is currently providing investors with a more affordable entry point into the growing natural gas midstream industry than its peer.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Kinder Morgan continues to be very well-positioned to profit from the growing demand for natural gas. The company is frequently thought of as a crude oil pipeline operator, but in fact, natural gas accounts for the overwhelming majority of its revenues, and it is likely that it will end up accounting for an even greater proportion of the company’s revenues and cash flows going forward. Thus, the company offers at least some of the exposure to the growing natural gas midstream segment that The Williams Companies offers at a lower price.

data.title: Bank Of America: Strong Investment Setup
data.description:
Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) reported higher-than-expected earnings for its third fiscal quarter, which were boosted by consumer banking as well as investment banking results. Bank of America continued to benefit from strong economic growth in the U.S. economy as well as falling inflation which has supported consumer spending. As a result, Bank of America reported strong results, especially for its consumer banking division, which saw double-digit earnings growth year-over-year. The bank grew its earnings to $8.5B in Q3'25 and raised its net interest income outlook for Q4'25. Despite falling federal fund rates and growing interest rate risk, shares of Bank of America remain a buy for investors with a positive view on the U.S. economy, in my opinion.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Bank of America a buy in July 2025 -- A Top Bank Buy For 2025 -- as the bank benefited from strong economic fundamentals in the U.S. economy and put up strong performance numbers, boosted by the firm's trading operations. In the third-quarter, Bank of America generated significant profitability as well and the bank passed the 2025 Federal Reserve stress test with flying colors during the summer… which clears the path for higher capital returns in the future. Although shares of Bank of America are trading at a P/B ratio above the historical average, I see a favorable macro backdrop and upside revaluation potential.

Bank of America beat Q3 estimates, strong NII performance

Bank of America reported higher-than-expected revenue and earnings for the third-quarter on Wednesday: the Wall Street powerhouse earned $1.06 per-share in adjusted earnings, beating the average estimate by $0.11 per-share. Revenues came in at $28.1B, surpassing the analyst consensus estimate by $630M.

Bank of America generated 28.1B in revenue in the third fiscal quarter, showing 11% year-over-year growth amid strong results especially in the consumer bank. In terms of profitability, the bank booked $8.5B in earnings in Q3'25, showing 23% year-over-year growth. This growth was boosted in large part by higher net interest income which is a key income source for large Wall Street banks.

Bank of America achieved strong results in its consumer bank, which generated 7% year-over-year revenue growth, mainly due to higher interest income (which I will discuss in the next section). The consumer bank is highly profitable for Bank of America and contributed $3.4B in net income (+28% Y/Y), and represented about 41% of consolidated earnings in the September quarter. Consumer banking benefits from a strong economic backdrop -- U.S. economic growth for Q2'25 was revised upwards by 0.5 PP to 3.8% -- as well as lower inflation which has backed up consumer spending.

Besides a strong performance in consumer banking, investment banking results, which are included in Bank of America's Global Banking unit, shone in the third-quarter: investment banking fees surged 43% year-over-year to $2.0B amid a flurry of investment deals and strong equity market performance throughout Q3.

Another area of interest, as usual with Wall Street banks that carry large amount of floating-rate loans on their balance sheets, is Bank of America's net interest income performance... and the bank did very well here: in the third-quarter, the Wall Street bank generated $15.4B in net interest income, showing a material increase of 9% year-over-year. This growth was mainly driven by fixed-rate asset repricing as well as higher deposit and loan balances in Bank of America's portfolio.

Because of a strong NII performance in Q3’25 and slow rate decreases on the part of the Federal Reserve, the bank raised its outlook for the fourth-quarter as well: Bank of America now sees $15.6-15.7B in net interest income (+8% Y/Y) for Q4'25, showing a $100M increased at the low-end of guidance compared to the previous outlook.

The Federal Reserve has guided for more rate cuts, however, which could pressure the bank's loan portfolio and net interest income in the quarters ahead. After the 25 basis point rate cut in September, the federal fund rate range has dropped to 4.00-4.25%. Inflation is currently running at less than 3% which helps make the case for more federal fund rate cuts in the coming quarters.

Bank of America’s valuation

Bank of America is the second-largest Bank in America by asset size, after JPMorgan Chase (JPM), and has been able to consistently grow its book value in the last ten years. In fact, BAC out-performed Wells Fargo (WFC), a key banking rival, by a considerable margin (~9 PP) over this time period.

JPM mainly gained book value due to a series of acquisitions, especially during periods of crisis, like the regional banking crisis in 2023. In the most recent quarter, Bank of America’s book value stood at $37.95 per-share, showing a year-over-year growth rate of 7.3%.

Bank of America shares are currently trading at a price-to-book ratio of 1.40X which makes them about 19% more expensive than they were on average in the last 5 years. Shares of Wells Fargo are trading at a price-to-book ratio of 1.65X. JPMorgan Chase, the world’s largest banking franchise, is trading at a P/B ratio of 2.45X.

If Bank of America were to revalue to the industry group average P/B ratio of 1.83X, shares could have a fair value in the neighborhood of ~$68, implying up to 31% upside revaluation potential.

Risks with Bank of America

Although BAC raised its NII outlook for Q4'25 amid positive growth in deposit and loan balances, a longer term risk factor relates to the federal fund trajectory because lower interest rates typically translate to lower loan rates as well… causing banks like Bank of America to make less money on their loan products. What would change my mind about Bank of America is if the bank were to see a drastic drop in its net interest income next year, which would be contingent on the Federal Reserve accelerating its rate-cutting trajectory.

Final thoughts

Bank of America submitted a better-than-expected earnings report card for the September quarter on Wednesday, which was defined by double-digit earnings growth compared to the year-earlier period. High single-digit NII growth and strong results in its consumer banking division + investment banking were responsible for the bank's solid earnings performance. Further, Bank of America raised its net interest income outlook for Q4'25 which highlights continual earnings growth potential. I like that Bank of America is able to grow its book value consistently and is seeing strong performance in its core business, consumer banking. With the economic backdrop in the U.S. broadly being favorable -- strong economic growth, falling inflation -- I believe the investment setup is still very solid for investors with a favorable long term view on the financial industry.

data.title: Enbridge: Latest Dividend Declaration Reflects Near-Term Pressure
data.description:
ENB stock’s latest dividend declaration

I last covered Enbridge Inc. (NYSE:ENB) stock on July 15 with an article entitled “Enbridge: $100 Oil Price Can Be A Black Swan In H2 2025 (Rating Upgrade).” The article focused on the geopolitics that can impact oil prices (CL1:COM) and ended with a Buy rating for the stock.

Since then, a new development is worth noting: the latest dividend declaration from the stock. More specifically, the chart provided below summarizes Enbridge’s recent dividend payouts. As seen, the latest declared regular dividend is $0.6825 per share for the August quarter. This represents a 1.3% QoQ increase compared to the previous payment of $0.6737, which had an Ex-Div Date of May 15. On a YOY basis, the latest $0.6825 payment marks an increase of 2.2% when compared to the $0.6678 dividend paid in August 2024.

Given the importance of dividends in ENB’s total return profile and also the company’s long record of paying/growing dividends, I consider its recent growth rate a good reflection of the near-term challenges facing the company, as detailed next.

ENB stock faces near term growth challenge

To provide a better context for this discussion, the next chart shows the historical dividend growth rates for Enbridge across different timeframes. As seen, the company’s 3-Year Dividend Growth Rate (CAGR) is even lower at 0.01%.Over the 5-Year (CAGR) period, the growth rate is at 2.55%, in stark contrast to the 10-Year CAGR of 6.49%.

Also note that ENB has enjoyed an average growth rate that is significantly higher than the sector median of in the past 10 years (by more than 6 folds), but its growth has lagged the sector median substantially in the recent 3~5 years. Judging by its latest payout declaration, I am afraid that the lag could persist in the near terms for a few reasons. The leading factor in my view is the challenges related to tariff discussions, which could delay new contract starts. Furthermore, uncertainties such as OPEC decision and the risk of China-U.S. trade disputes could keep pressuring commodity prices in the energy sector.

However, these issues are ultimately temporary in my model. For longer-term investors who can look past these issues, ENB provides a well-rounded package of yield, growth, and value as detailed next.

ENB stock: long-term growth outlook

The next chart shows the EPS estimates for Enbridge over the next few fiscal years (FY 2025 to FY 2031). As seen, the company is forecasted to experience healthy earnings growth, with a potential average growth rate of 9.6% CAGR during this period. Its EPS is projected to rise from $2.15 in FY 2025 to $3.73 in FY 2031. In tandem, the implied forward P/E ratios are expected to drop steadily and considerably from 21.95 in 2025 to only 12.65 in 2031.

I do see plenty of long-term tailwinds at work for Enbridge that can support the above EPS growth projection. To start, ENB is expanding storage capacity near the Ingleside facility to support the growth of the offshore projects with BP in the Gulf Coast. Next, I also expect the rapid expansion of AI, data centers, and cloud computing to provide a significant secular demand for its services and products. These computer hubs command large amounts of energy both for operation and also cooling purposes. Admittedly, a good portion of the energy investments related to these digital technologies are geared towards renewable energy instead of fossil fuels. However, ENB is quite active on the renewable front too and has made significant progress lately.

A notable example is the recent $900 million solar project with Meta (known as the Clear Fork project) to be built near San Antonio. More details of this project are quoted below:

Construction for the project has already begun and is expected to be complete by summer 2027. The power produced by the new plant is set to supply Meta's data centers, which are continuously in need of clean power. In total, Enbridge expects the project to cost around $900 million.

Other risks and final thoughts

As another catalyst, the company has recently completed three gas utility acquisitions. These purchases provided additional throughput capacity and notable contributions to its revenues on a yearly basis. I feel the timing of the additional capacity is also fortuitous given the colder-than-normal weather in Ontario this year, which helps to drive higher gas distribution demand and higher transportation fees.

On the negative side, the associated financing costs and maintenance have further stretched the company’s balance sheet, a factor that investors should monitor closely. As an example, the chart below shows Enbridge's balance sheet with a focus on its long-term and total liabilities. As seen, both have increased substantially in the past 2~3 years. To wit, as of December 2023, the long-term and total liabilities stood at $56.4 bn and $87.4 bn, respectively. In less than 3 years, these figures have grown to $71.2 bn and $105.2 bn as of its last report. The increase of its long-term debt is a main factor of its liability.

To close, this ENB revisit is triggered by its latest dividend declaration. Given the role dividends play in ENB’s investment thesis, I consider it as a reflection of the near-term challenges the company is facing, ranging from tariff uncertainties, oil price pressure, borrowing/financing costs, etc. However, these challenges are ultimately temporary in my view. For investors with a longer timeframe (say 3~5 years), my verdict is that outside risks outweigh the downside risks given the growth catalysts afoot and the secular demand tailwinds.

data.title: Automatic Data Processing Q1 Preview: Might Be More Downside To Come
data.description:
Introduction

Automatic Data Processing, Inc. (NASDAQ:ADP) is due to announce its Q1 ’26 earnings on the 29th of October, so I wanted to go through what to expect and give some comments on what I’m looking for from the report and the rest of the year in terms of outlook. Recent share price performance hasn’t been very good, and I expect there could be more downside if the report is anything but predictable, especially if the management decides to update the company’s outlook for the next year.

What to Expect

Analysts are expecting the company will make around $2.45 in earnings, on $5.14B in sales. Zero sequential growth in sales and around 6% growth y/y. The company has been chugging along at this pace for a while now, with no significant growth in operations, which makes it quite easy to estimate what it will do over the upcoming quarters.

And how do these estimates stack up against what the management expects the company to do? Unfortunately, ADP’s management isn’t a fan of providing quarterly forecasts and sticks mostly to full-year guidance numbers, which the management said the company will manage to achieve 5% to 6% sales growth, which is slightly below the average of analysts’ expectations of around 5.7% (very close though).

Since the company’s performance is quite strong and predictable, I don’t expect much deviation from analysts’ expectations on October 29th. We can see EPS and sales numbers are very close to what the company achieves every quarter. So, I do not expect a massive surprise.

What I’m Looking For

With the uneasiness and volatility building up in the U.S., with the government shutdown in full force, which is on its way to being the longest shutdown in a while, if that is how the situation is progressing right now, I am going to be very interested in whether the management’s tone will be somewhat concerning. Everyone will be expecting some sort of update to the figures already provided. Granted, a lot of the time, if a company just provided outlook numbers last quarter, it would hardly do it again unless it is completely necessary due to some unforeseen changes in the economy. So, an update on the previous numbers would be a sign of trouble if they are going to be updated to the downside. The numbers are already a little behind analysts’ expectations.

The unemployment rate has been steady at around 4.1% to 4.3%, as there has been very little data change from prior months. However, job growth has slowed, as well as job openings compared to last year, with some reporting of decreases in job openings anywhere from 5.5% to 8.9%. And now with the shutdown in full swing, there will be no data to rely on, which will most likely make a lot of economic decisions much harder to predict. Especially regarding interest rates.

For the rest of the year, economists expect to see further labor market deterioration leading to modest job creation and some further rise in the unemployment rate to 4.5%.

I want to see if the management will have any comments regarding the economy, and I’m sure they will, because people want some answers. The government shutdown isn’t helping. Companies like ADP need to step up and provide some sort of light and show what they see in the economy.

Regarding the company’s operations, I would like to get an update on the company’s PEO segment growth outlook, which remains the key revenue driver. Additionally, I will pay attention to how AI has helped the company stay ahead of the competition and what kind of benefits the company is yet to see in the future. With the creation of a Chief Product and AI Officer last year, the company is all in on AI, which should be well aligned with its HCM segment and expertise. I don’t expect AI to play a role in bringing the company to new levels of growth, but rather help it maintain its mid-single-digit growth.

Updates on its AI-driven features in its core products, like Workforce Now, ADP Global Payroll, and Lyric HCM, which aim to reduce manual HR admin work, improve payroll accuracy, and speed up analytics. I wonder what kind of reception these AI features received from the clients, as well as prospective clients.

The company expects its Employer Services revenues to grow at around 5%-6%, while the booking of new business grows between 4%-7%. I will be looking out for any changes in these numbers, which could indicate that there may be troubles ahead if they are updated to the downside.

Closing Comments

I don’t think it is a good time to start a position right before earnings, especially with how the economy is so unpredictable right now. The company has been quite resilient and has been trading at a premium for this reason; however, the latest share price performance isn’t very promising. The company’s share price is quite a bit away from its recent highs set in June, which could be a good time to add if you are a long-term investor, but I don’t think the downside is all priced in yet. I wouldn’t be surprised to see further downside pressure over the remainder of the year.

data.title: CAT: AI Bubble Or Just Optimistic?
data.description:
Having just written about a parabolic trend in the VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF (NLR), I instantly recognized another one in an unexpected place: Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT).

CAT used to be a slow and steady stock that would take two steps forward and one back. NLR used to plod along, too. But AI has changed that, and you can see its effect as both trends have steepened at the same time.

This is the energy and infrastructure build phase of AI. CAT doesn't have much relation to nuclear energy or NRL, but AI needs power to run, and CAT is deeply involved. This is a key phrase from the Q2 earnings call:

Power Generation grew by 19%, primarily due to demand for reciprocating engines for data center applications.

Energy and transportation was the only segment to report growth in Q2 earnings and had the highest revenue.

When the largest segment is growing by 7% and likely to grow more over a sustained period, it overshadows the weaker segments. At its heart, CAT is still a cyclical stock, but the AI-fueled growth is balancing that out. When the cyclical segments swing back, it could be firing on all cylinders. But is that too optimistic?

Is It a Bubble?

There is a lot of speculation on whether AI is creating a stock market bubble like the late '90s. The graphic below is a good illustration of why it is not the same, at least in semiconductor stocks like Nvdia (NVDA), which are growing earnings.

Here is another interesting chart which illustrates that earnings growth has contributed much more to the S&P 500 (SPY) rally than multiple expansion has.

Earnings are growing strongly, and the stock market is not in a bubble due to AI.

But CAT is not NVDA or SPY. Earnings have disappointed and even dropped in 2025.

The rally this year has therefore been largely driven by margin expansion.

EPS is forecast to grow 15% in Q2 next year, although the comparisons from this year make double-digit growth relatively easy. In reality, EPS would only be recovering to $5.40, which is back at 2024 levels.

Perhaps the AI build will drive sustained double-digit growth, but this is both optimistic and seems to be getting priced in already. It's interesting that, as some areas of the AI megatrend are resting, CAT (and utilities/NRL) have accelerated higher. It seems like the same money is driving moves in different areas at different times.

CAT has had a particularly strong performance in October and was helped by a note from Oppenheimer which made it their top pick.

Oppenheimer highlighted a strong uptrend in AI-driven capital expenditure, noting that hyperscaler capex forecasts for 2025–2028 have risen 23% quarter-over-quarter...

The firm called CAT its top pick, citing strong infrastructure demand, stable mining trends, and pricing leverage in energy and transportation.

If other segments could also recover, earnings growth could be solid. Despite the weak earnings in Q2, the outlook looks positive, and the earnings call highlighted a record backlog.

As I mentioned, backlog increased sequentially by $2.5 billion, driven by strong order rates in all three of our primary segments. Our backlog is at a record level of $37.5 billion.

But let's not pretend that is the reason for the huge surge in CAT's price and valuation. This is AI optimism, which is partially merited, but likely over the top.

The Wall of Worry

Powerful buyers are focusing on the positives such as AI. For the rest of us, there is plenty to worry about: overvaluation is one major concern, but tariffs are also a black cloud that may keep investors away. Tariffs were mentioned 41 times on the earnings call, and are having a significant impact.

Based on the incremental tariffs announced in 2025 and expected to be placed on August the 7th, we expect the net impact from incremental tariffs for 2025 will be around $1.3 to $1.5 billion, net of some mitigating actions and cost controls.

But here's the rub: President Trump floated the idea of a 100% tariff last Friday and brought the trade war sharply back in focus. Meanwhile, CAT is having its best week of 2025 with a gain of 10% (so far). CAT is climbing the wall of worry and shrugging off all the concerns. It seems illogical, but that's often the way markets work.

Realistic Expectations

While I won't go as far as to say CAT is in an AI bubble, the current rally is pricing in a lot of good news, and that means good news or good earnings won't necessarily have much impact.

It also means that any bad news could have an oversized effect. I would not buy the stock here, especially with Q3 earnings just ahead on October 29. The report is not expected to show any great improvements over Q2 and may be a reminder that CAT, for all its potential, is not growing in two of its three segments.

CAT would be attractive if it were to enter a sustained consolidation or correction, as earnings could catch up and lower its valuation. That could take some time.

Conclusions

CAT has accelerated higher just when the broader market is falling and tariffs are back in focus. This is related to optimism over AI infrastructure building - spending on data centers keeps increasing, and CAT's energy and transport segment is benefiting and showing healthy growth.

Some optimism is merited, and I would not yet call the rally a bubble. On the other hand, I don't think a doubling of the PE ratio is justified or healthy. I would not buy CAT here, but would be interested if the price stalls and earnings live up to expectations in H1 2026.

data.title: A New Trade War Begun? U.S.-China Tensions Back In The Spotlight
data.description:
By Krzysztof Kamiński

In recent weeks, trade tensions between the United States and China have visibly intensified, raising fears of a new wave of economic conflict between the world’s two largest economies. Rhetoric on both sides has become increasingly confrontational, and the growing risk of decoupling and disruptions to supply chains could have far-reaching consequences for the global economy.

China Responds with Sanctions – Tensions After Madrid Talks

The immediate catalyst for the escalation was the round of talks in Madrid, after which the United States expanded sanctions against companies linked to Chinese entities on the so-called “blacklist.” In response, Beijing announced sanctions against American firms cooperating with South Korean company Hanwha — including Philly Shipyard, which fulfills contracts for the U.S. Navy. Chinese authorities justified the move as a countermeasure to the deepening defense cooperation between the U.S. and South Korea.

Negotiation Climate Weakens, But Formal Frameworks Remain

China’s Minister of Commerce, Wang Wentao, accused the U.S. of “destroying the constructive atmosphere for negotiations” and called for an immediate withdrawal from what he termed “erroneous trade practices.” At the same time, China announced it would release a report within the WTO evaluating U.S. actions in 11 areas of international trade.

Meanwhile, U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent warned that China’s restrictions on the export of rare earth metals could accelerate the global process of economic decoupling — the effort to reduce dependency on Chinese supply chains.

Trump: 100% Tariffs Possible, But Undesirable

President Donald Trump signaled that 100% tariffs on Chinese goods were possible if tensions continue to escalate, though he sought to calm markets by noting that “both leaders do not want a global recession.” The planned Trump–Xi meeting during the upcoming APEC summit in South Korea could prove decisive for the future of bilateral relations.

Trade Dispute Deepens China’s Economic Troubles

The escalation comes at a difficult moment for China’s economy. Despite record exports (trade surplus: USD 875 billion), GDP growth in Q3 is projected at just 4.7% year-on-year — potentially the weakest result in a year.

Domestic indicators are also disappointing:

  • Retail sales (September): +3% y/y – the weakest result in 2025
  • Industrial production: +5%, with slowing momentum
  • Fixed-asset investment: near zero growth
  • Foreign direct investment: down 13% over the past eight months
  • Real estate sector: still in recession, with deflation for the ninth consecutive quarter

Government Support Falls Short

The Chinese government has launched a 500-billion-yuan investment package, focused mainly on infrastructure. However, a large share of the funds has gone toward repaying previous obligations, limiting the actual stimulus effect.

Ahead of the upcoming plenum of the Chinese Communist Party, new measures to boost consumption are expected. Household consumption currently accounts for only 40% of GDP — well below the global average of 56% and the roughly 60% level typical for developed economies.

A New Wave of Trade War?

Further escalation could trigger a new round of trade warfare, hitting global supply chains, access to strategic resources, and financial market stability. Although the formal negotiation framework established in London remains in place, sentiment on both sides is increasingly confrontational.

While a full-scale trade war is not yet inevitable, the current escalation signals that U.S.–China relations are entering a new phase of uncertainty. High-level meetings and potential decisions on further restrictions remain at the center of global attention. For the world economy, this is a test of resilience in a new geopolitical reality — one in which trade is no longer just about economics.
