data.title: AbbVie: "Strong Buy" On Rinvoq Expansions And Immunology Pipeline Addition
data.description:
The last time I wrote about AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV) was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "AbbVie: Skyrizi And Rinvoq Latest FDA Approvals Bode Well For Humira Patent Loss". With this respect to this article, I noted that Humira was approaching the loss of patents and that both Skyrizi and Rinvoq [upadacitinib] would pick up the mantle and carry sales for the company going forward. This is exactly what happened as both of these drugs have been doing quite well in terms of revenues. That is, sales of Skyrizi and Rinvoq sales increased by 62.2% to $4.423 billion and 41.8% to $2.028 billion, respectively. Speaking of Rinvoq, this drug has come a long way and what I want to provide as updates below for this JAK inhibitor candidate is that it was able to achieve positive results from a second pivotal phase 3 study, known as Study 1.

This is part of the entire phase 3 UP-AA program that had both of these two replicative studies to evaluate the use of Rinvoq for the treatment of adolescent and adult patients with severe alopecia areata [AA]. The primary endpoint of this study was met in a statistically significant manner with both 15 mg and 30 mg doses of this drug targeting this group of patients. The beauty of this is that AbbVie intends to submit this data to regulators across the globe in order to notch another FDA approval for it. The truth is that the company continues to expand its approval of this oral JAK inhibitor. For instance, another update that happened in the most recent second-quarter of 2025 was achieving the first ever FDA approval Rinvoq for the treatment of adults with giant cell arteritis [GCA]. While it is expanding this particular drug towards other indications, another update I want to go over is the continued building of its immunology pipeline.

While Skyrizi and Rinvoq are strong, it is already planning for the future with a clinical candidate it obtained through the acquisition of Capstan Therapeutics, known as CPTX2309. This clinical candidate provides a differentiated mode of action of going after B-cell mediated autoimmune disorders. It is a targeted lipid nanoparticle [tLNP] that delivers an mRNA encoding an anti-CD19 chimeric antigen receptor [CAR] capable of generating CD19-specific and CD8+ in vivo CAR-T cells that are capable of depleting B-cells. Most importantly, the ability to treat such patients without any need of prior lymphodepleting chemotherapy agents. With Rinvoq expansions happening recently, plus the acquisition of a potential first-in-class tLNP candidate capable of resetting the immune system in patients with B-cell mediated autoimmune disorders, I believe that AbbVie should remain as a "Strong Buy" rating.

Two Positive Developments For Rinvoq Bode Well For Long-Term Revenue Growth

As I noted above, AbbVie was able to achieve two key positive developments as part of its expansion of its oral JAK inhibitor candidate Rinvoq. The first of such expansions that happened in the second quarter of 2025 was that it was able to achieve a "first" in terms of FDA approval. That is, this drug became the first JAK inhibitor to be approved by the FDA for the treatment of patients with giant cell arteritis [GCA]. This disorder is classified as inflammation that occurs on the lining of arteries on the temporal lobe of a patient. What makes this disorder devastating is that there is no actual known cause of it. Besides causing some symptoms like headaches and jaw pain, this condition gets worse if not treated promptly. How so? Well, if not treated then the inflamed arteries can cause the person to lose their vision in one or both of their eyes. GCA is classified as an autoimmune disorder and as such it occurs because the immune system attacks the blood vessels of the patient. Rinvoq helps these patients by specifically blocking key inflammatory pathways inside of immune cells. Some actions of importance would be the blocking of inflammatory cytokines and activation of JAKs. In essence, the goal is to block the inflammatory response that is activated and ends up damaging the blood vessel walls causing inflammation. The reason for the drug being approved was that the primary endpoint of the phase 3 SELECT-GCA trial, sustained remission at week 52 was met in a statistically significant manner. Rinvoq was compared to placebo on the basis of this primary endpoint. The thing is that the trial recruited patients into each group as follows:

  • 209 patients who received 15 mg of Rinvoq
  • 107 patients who received 7.5 mg of Rinvoq
  • 112 patients who received placebo

The final outcome was that the higher dose of 15 mg of Rinvoq achieved this primary endpoint, in that 46.4% of patients had sustained remission through week 52 compared to 29% of patients on placebo [statistically significant p-value, p=0.002]. The beauty of this approval is that it marks the ninth one for Rinvoq.

The second positive update to make note of would be that the company has a shot at being able to expand the use of Rinvoq towards another indication soon enough. How so? Well, that's because in August of 2025 it announced positive results from Study 1 as part of its phase 3 UP-AA clinical trial program using Rinvoq to treat adult and adolescent patients with severe alopecia areata [AA]. Previous to this, it reported a positive outcome as well for this program, but in Study 2. In this particular trial portion as part of this program it noted that 44.6% and 54.3% of patients, which took 15 mg and 30 mg of Rinvoq had 80% or more scalp hair coverage at week 24 , defined by SALT score ≤ 20. With both studies being equivalent, I believe it is important to go over the trial data from the more recent one in detail, which was Study 1. The other trial, Study 1 also had patients given Rinvoq that ended up seeing 80% or higher scalp hair coverage at Week 24 compared to placebo. The efficacy outcome percentage of patients that reached this goal over a 24-week period compared to placebo are noted below:

  • Patients that took 15 mg of Rinvoq : 45.2% achieved this primary endpoint
  • Patients that took 30 mg of Rinvoq : 55% achieved this primary endpoint
  • Placebo comparator : 1.5%

Should this drug be approved to treat these patients, it will be a welcomed change compared to current treatment options, which are plagued with uncertainty for starters. Plus, the mode of action of Rinvoq is ideal, as in the data release it was noted that hair regrowth was observed both in terms of scalp and non-scalp measures. Another factor to consider is that when patients entered into this study for screening, they had an average baseline SALT score of 84, which translates to 16% of scalp hair coverage. To have a good chunk of patients with hair regrowth and be offered a new treatment option such as this oral JAK inhibitor could be ideal for them. The next step gets even better for AbbVie because it will immediately submit this data from both of these pivotal studies to regulators. This means that the company could achieve another indication under its belt for Rinvoq if it is ultimately approved by the FDA to treat adolescents and adults with severe AA.

Acquisition Of Capstan Brings Forth Candidate To Potentially Reset Immune system in Autoimmune Disorders

Another positive development I want to go over involves the fact that AbbVie spent $2.1 billion to acquire Capstan Therapeutics. The reason for doing so was so that it could obtain potential first-in-class targeted lipid nanoparticle [tLNP] anti-CD-19 CAR-T therapy to target patients with B-cell mediated disorders. With this acquisition, it obtains the ability to advance the use of CPTX2309 to help with targeting these patients. What makes this candidate, plus the technology of Capstan Therapeutics so highly ideal, is that there is in vivo programming of the CAR. This is a very good thing because of the programming being done in this fashion, there is no need for prior lymphodepletion before providing patients with CPTX2309. The way that this candidate works is by delivering a messenger RNA [mRNA] payload of an anti-CD-19 CAR to cytotoxic CD8+ T cells and thus programming takes place in an in vivo fashion. These reprogrammed CD8+ T cells now have the component to deplete B-cells, which have the CD-19 protein on their cell's surface.

That is, this is a hallmark component of B-cell mediated autoimmune disorders. The bottom line is that this offers a new scope of CAR T targeting, whereby the new modality of in vivo engineering of cells provides an excellent way to have off-the-shelf capability. This brings me to the final point, which is that the company not only obtains CPTX2309 as a candidate, but also gains the use of Capstan's tLNP platform technology. The point is that any RNA payload can likely be used to engineer cells in an in vivo fashion. Besides being a novel mode of action, it provides the ability to do what I stated beforehand, which is to reset the immune system. Furthermore, achieving this generates a scope far beyond only targeting the symptoms of autoimmune disorders, but also targeting the underlying cause of them. In essence, CPTX2309 and the tLNP platform technology can have a mandate to beyond what may have been achieved with candidates in immunology like Rinvoq and Skyrizi so far. Not only that, but it could change the treatment paradigm moving forward as some CAR-T therapies are modified ex vivo [outside of the body]. Being able to achieve programming in vivo could provide far more advantages than just off-the-shelf capability alone. To that end, the acquisition of Capstan Therapeutics was completed on August 19, 2025.

Financials

AbbVie is doing quite well with respect to earnings and it continues to see growth in revenues, which is a good thing. For instance, in Q2 of 2052 it reported global net revenues of $15.42 billion, which is an increase of 6.6% year over year. The truth is that the company has well established growth beyond what I went over above with respect to its immunology portfolio. Where it saw the best overall growth in a year-over-year fashion was instead with its neuroscience portfolio. In other words, this would be the targeting of neurological disorders. Consider that the neuroscience portfolio grew by 24.2% to $2.683 billion. Drugs in this specific category are: Vraylar, Botox, Qulipta, and Ubrelvy. The clear winner though, believe it or not, was Botox from here which saw revenue growth to $928 million.

This ended up being a year-over-year growth of 14.1%. The immunology portfolio is still growing at 9% year over year, but a problem that remains is a decline in sales for Humira which continues to decrease and in the most recent Q2 of 2025, dropped in sales by 58.1% to $1.180 billion. If not for this, the category would be higher. Still, it goes back to what I have shown above, that this immunology portfolio can continue to grow thanks to new indication approvals for Rinvoq for starters. What would also end up helping out is if tLNP CPTX2309 is successfully advanced through all clinical testing and ultimately improved. I believe it would be huge because of the ability to provide off-the-shelf CAR-Ts and ones that won't require lymphodepletion for the patient in question.

AbbVie is expected to report Q3 of 2025 financial results on Friday October 31, 2025 before the market opens. The expectation is that Q3 earnings per share [EPS] is expected to achieve year-over-year growth, but full overall year-adjusted EPS guidance has been lowered. What's the reason for this? It has been lowered to a range to be between $10.38 - $10.58 and it is because of a host of intangible assets due to a merger or transaction. The key expectation and what is important to watch for would be the neuroscience portfolio as I described above, which saw revenue grow by 24.2%. Total year-over-year revenue growth is expected to be higher, forecasted by analysts to be around 6.6% during the quarter.

AbbVie's valuation in terms of price-to-earnings [P/E] ratio is abnormally high at a trailing 12-month basis of 103.26, compared to its own peers of 21.37x and the biotech industry average P/E in the United States of 15.77x. However, I want to note that the expectation for this company is not based on what its current P/E ratio is set at. In essence, it is largely based on the promise of its future growth drivers that it has in place. With the forward P/E of AbbVie being 16.84 as the basis for future growth. The expansion of its immunology portfolio is one promising aspect, where there is an expectation for expansion based on moving towards other indications. In addition, there is the acquisition of Capstan Therapeutics, which brings forth CPTX2309 as a targeted lipid nanoparticle [tLNP] and anti-CD19 CAR-T therapy candidate to target patients with B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders. This is one promise of expanding its immunology portfolio. There is room for future growth here, as the immunology portfolio at the moment is growing with revenues at 9.5% year over year. However, the best expectation of its future growth driver would be the best-growing portfolio it has, which is the neuroscience one. How so? Well, this specific portfolio saw revenue growth by 24.2% year-over-year to $2.683 billion. The point here is that even though the P/E ratio is high, investors are investing based on the expectation that the company has a lot of growth drivers in place. Thus, while the P/E is higher than it once was historically, there is still room for it to grow its earnings based on future prospects. I believe that the valuation is worth is based on its strategy of seeing growth in several different portfolios. Of course, being 22 higher in P/E than its peers means a huge valuation risk if the expectations of its ongoing trials and expected expansions are not met.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in AbbVie. The first risk to consider is the immunology portfolio with respect to Rinvoq and more broadly. The first risk to consider would be the latest approval of this drug for the treatment of patients with GCA. The reason why is because even with this approval there is no guarantee that it will cause revenues for Rinvoq to pick up because of it. Secondly, the goal is to get the AA indication as well for this drug. I believe that, if approved, this could actually change the scope of revenues to be made with this drug. Why is that? Consider that the global Alopecia areata market is expected to reach $16.02 billion by 2030. The risk here is that despite the primary endpoints being met in both Study 1 and Study 2, there is no assurance that the FDA is ultimately going to grant marketing approval of Rinvoq for this specific indication.

The second risk to consider would be declining sales of Humira. As I indicated in my prior article, the company was set to meet a major problem with declining sales for this drug. This continues to be the case and in the most recent Q2 of 2025 earnings, is recognized with a decline in sales by 58.1% to $1.180 billion. The good news is that AbbVie has Skyrizi and Rinvoq in place to counter the lost revenues with Humira. Going forward, I think such deals like acquiring companies to add as part of its immunology portfolio may end up paying off.

Speaking of which, this brings forth the third and final risk to consider here, which would be the latest acquisition of Capstan Therapeutics. The reason why is because the in vivo targeted lipid nanoparticle [tLNP] anti-CD-19 therapy is currently being explored in a phase 1 study targeting patients with B-cell mediated autoimmune disorders. The risk here is that despite the belief that in vivo programming of CD8 expressing cytotoxic T cells will allow for the depletion of B-cells in an effective and durable manner without lymphodepletion, this may not end up being the case. There is no guarantee that the results to be released from the phase 1 study are going to be positive for starters. Nor that the data to be produced from this early-stage study is going to be enough for AbbVie to want to continue to advance this program for phase 2 clinical testing.

Conclusion

AbbVie has been able to achieve some expansions this year in terms of its drug Rinvoq. The first of which, noted above, would be the FDA approval of this oral JAK inhibitor for the treatment of patients with GCA. Beyond the scope of this regulatory approval, I believe that the stronger indication would be the completion of the two late-stage studies as part of the phase 3 AA-UP program, with the targeting of patients with AA. As I have shown above, Rinvoq was able to allow 44.6% and 54.3% of patients, who took 15 mg and 30 mg of Rinvoq, to have 80% or more scalp hair coverage at week 24. This is compared to the low 1.5% of patients that attained this efficacy outcome measure who were given placebo instead.

The bottom line here is that I believe it is still bullish that AbbVie is looking to continue growth in its immunology portfolio. Especially since it is still reeling from the loss of its Humira patents. The addition of tLNP CPTX2309 could end up changing how CAR T therapies work in treating patients with B-cell mediated autoimmune disorders. That is, to alter the CAR T landscape with the scope of in vivo programming instead of ex vivo [outside of the body] modification. Lastly, it is not relying on its immunology portfolio for growth at all. As I have shown above in the financials section, it was able to see double-digit percentage growth of 24.2% to $2.683 billion in Q2 of 2025. Based on everything I have stated, I believe that AbbVie's stock should be kept with a "Strong Buy" rating.

data.title: 3 Price Catalysts For Eli Lilly
data.description:
When I last wrote about the obesity treatments' specialist Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) in August, there was a 50% upside to the stock following its crash after disappointing drug trial results. In the two months since, the stock has realised ~30% of the upside far exceeding the gains for sector, as seen from the 9% gains for the Health Care Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLV).

Clearly, there are still some 20% gains to be made, with no updates on the earnings outlook since. But there are also catalysts for the stock underway, which are likely to support a price uptick for LLY, but can also pose risks. Here are three of them.

#1. Agreement with the US government

The threat of 100% tariffs on imported pharmaceutical products has been a key challenge looming over the sector this year. But with Pfizer's (PFE) deal with the US government at the end of September, and Anglo-Swedish cancer treatments' provider AstraZeneca's (AZN) agreement last week, the risk has reduced substantially.

Pfizer is expected to invest some $70 billion in the US, in lieu of which, it gets a grace period of three years on tariffs. Its understanding with the government also entails lowering drug prices by 50% for patients in the US. AstraZeneca came to a similar agreement.

Since these agreements, LLY has jumped by ~14%. With Eli Lilly speculated to make a deal soon as well, the stock could rise even further, especially as it has already decided to invest $50 billion in the US in the foreseeable future as well. With this investment amount, it's likely to get a relaxation on tariffs as well. As and when the agreement comes through, the stock can rally even more.

#2. Earnings outlook at potential risk

While broadly speaking, an agreement with the US government will be positive for LLY, it does risk reducing margins for Eli Lilly. After all, Pfizer's deal entails selling medicines to US based patients at par with prices in other developed countries, which translates into reducing them.

A similar expectation is expected to be placed on Eli Lilly. In fact, a Seeking Alpha report has said that Ozempic's price would be reduced by the government, which is a close enough indicator. Also this wouldn't be the first time that weight loss treatments' companies are coming under the government lens. Even last year, the Biden administration was critical of the price charged by both Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk (NVO).

Here's the twist in the tale, though. Despite reducing prices, the company's operating margin has only risen since. From an already high 39.1% in Q2 2024, the number had increased to 45.8% by Q2 2025. Whether it's able to pull off the same again remains to be seen. We could get an indication as early as October 30, when the company releases its Q3 2025 results. If the outlook is indeed reduced, the price upside will get diminished and could negatively impact the price.

#3. Trial results please investors

Next, at the time I last checked on the stock in August, results for the company's trial GLP-1 treatment, Orforglipron, had made investors unhappy. The stock saw a sharp 14% plunge on the day they were released since they weren't at par with Eli Lilly's highly successful current weight loss treatment, Zepbound.

However, even at that time, clear positives for Orforglipron were evident. First, there was the ease of administering it as a pill instead of an injectable. Second, it had a positive impact on other biomarkers. And third, it had the potential to be produced at scale (see segment "Mounjaro, Zepbound, and the case for Orforglipron" in the link for details).

The case for Orforglipron has only strengthened since, based on its impact for a narrower segment of overweight trial participants, who also have diabetes. In late August, the company said that such participants saw a 12.4% reduction in weight with a dosage of 36 mg daily after 72 weeks. The news resulted in ~6% jump in stock price on the day.

Subsequently, the treatment's diabetes related biomarkers also showed superior results compared with other oral competitors like oral semaglutide, which is marketed by Eli Novo Nordisk as Rybelsus. The biomarker A1C saw a 2.1% drop with Orforglipron, compared with a 1.4% decline with oral semaglutide at 52 weeks.

Earlier this week, the company also said that Orforglipron also showed better performance compared to dapagliflozin, which is sold by AstraZeneca as Fraxiga. At 40 weeks, it lowered A1C by 1.7% compared with dapagliflozin's 0.8%.

With the recent buildup around the treatment coupled with its potential to impact LLY's price, it can be yet another catalyst for the stock when another study on its outcomes becomes available in January 2026 (see slide below).

Looking beyond weight loss and diabetes

Outside of the key price catalysts, the company's recent advances in areas of treatment other than cardio-metabolic health also merit a mention. Specifically, progress cancer treatments are particularly notable here considering that Verzenio has already brought in some 9.4% of the revenues in H1 2025.

Its treatment for advanced or metastatic breast cancer, branded as Inluriyo, received an approval by the US FDA as it showed better outcomes than alternative therapies. This followed the FDA's approval for Verzenio for late stage breast cancer, which can improve survival in combination with other therapies. The company also reported positive trial results for its leukemia treatment Jaypirca last month.

They might not move the price for LLY immediately, but they are certainly worth keeping a lookout for.

What next?

In a nutshell, investors can look out for three potential price catalysts for LLY going forward. The first, which can happen anytime now, is an agreement with the Trump administration on tariffs, which can be positive.

Second, the Q3 2025 results due at the end of October, which can go either way. If they factor in any price reductions following an agreement with the government, the earnings outlook can be negatively impacted. Third, further trial results for Orforglipron can impact LLY's price, after it has moved the stock notably more than once since August.

On the whole, there's still more to look forward to with respect to Eli Lilly than not. And for that reason, I'm maintaining a Strong Buy rating on it for now. However, it could be reduced if there are negative developments regarding any of the three catalysts.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
